_comment: Source: NASEMSO National Model EMS Clinical Guidelines v3.0 (March 2022). Resuscitation: Adult Post-ROSC (Return of Spontaneous Circulation) Care. NASEMSO national provisional base.
level_display: All Levels — Interventions Scoped Per Provider Level
condition: Adult Post-ROSC (Return of Spontaneous Circulation) Care
protocol_reference: NASEMSO National Model EMS Clinical Guidelines v3.0 — Resuscitation: Adult Post-ROSC (Return of Spontaneous Circulation) Care

sections.title: Patient Care Goals
sections.reference: NASEMSO National Model EMS Clinical Guidelines v3.0 — Resuscitation: Adult Post-ROSC (Return of Spontaneous Circulation) Care
points: The immediate ROSC period is critical in stabilizing patients and preparing for transport. The goal is therefore to maximize survival and optimize neurologic and cardiovascular function following a return of spontaneous circulation by the following steps:
Secure airway
Obtain vascular access
Maximize blood pressure
Identify ST-elevation myocardial infarction (STEMI) or reversible causes of arrest
Recognize pending re-arrest
Consider appropriate destination choice

sections.title: Patient Presentation
points: Patient returned to spontaneous circulation following cardiac arrest resuscitation
EXCLUSION CRITERIA:
None noted

sections.title: EMR Interventions
sections.reference: NASEMSO v3.0 — EMR level (Emergency Medical Responder)
points: Scene safety and PPE; primary survey (ABCDE)
Oxygen via NRM targeting SpO2 94-98% if indicated
Position of comfort; hemorrhage control as needed
CPR/AED if patient becomes pulseless
Epinephrine auto-injector for anaphylaxis if applicable
Naloxone intranasal unit-dose for suspected opioid involvement

sections.title: EMT Interventions
sections.reference: NASEMSO v3.0 — EMT level (Emergency Medical Technician)
points: Blood glucose monitoring; oral glucose if hypoglycemic and alert
12-lead ECG acquisition and transmission
CPAP for respiratory distress
Aspirin for suspected ischemic chest pain
Albuterol SVN for bronchospasm component
IV access maintenance (non-medicated)

sections.title: AEMT Interventions
points: IV/IO access initiation
IV/IO fluid resuscitation as indicated
Waveform capnography
IV analgesics (fentanyl, morphine) per pain management protocol
IV antiemetics (ondansetron) per protocol
IV D50W/D10W for hypoglycemia

sections.title: Paramedic Interventions
points: 12-lead ECG interpretation
Endotracheal intubation for airway compromise
RSI per protocol
Advanced antidysrhythmic or hemodynamic support per specific protocol
All parenteral medications per protocol
Vasopressors for refractory hemodynamic compromise

sections.title: Patient Safety Considerations
points: Avoid hyperthermia (temperature greater than 37.5' C or 99.5' F) by avoiding excessive environmental heat exposure, warm blankets, etc.
a. Beyond interventions to prevent hyperthermia or fever, prehospital initiation of therapeutic hypothermia (targeted temperature management) is not routinely recommended

sections.title: Educational Pearls
points: Hyperventilation is a significant cause of hypotension and recurrence of cardiac arrest in the post resuscitation phase and must be avoided. Similarly, hypoventilation (suggested by an EtCO2 greater than 40'45) contributes to worsening acidosis and may precipitate re-arrest
Most patients are comatose immediately after resuscitation and will require airway management and ventilatory assistance

Many patients experience 'stunning' of the cardiac muscle after ROSC. Hypotension is common, and volume resuscitation or vasopressor support is often required. Refer to the [Shock Guideline] for further recommendations
Common non-cardiac causes of post-resuscitation hypotension include hyperventilation, hypovolemia, and traumatic pneumothorax from chest compressions
The condition of post-resuscitation patients fluctuates rapidly and continuously requiring close monitoring. A significant percentage of post-ROSC patients will re-arrest
Current research has demonstrated that care of patients with ROSC at specialized centers is associated with both decreased mortality and improved neurologic outcomes
Maintain mechanical CPR device in place in preparation for re-arrest

A moderate number of adult post-ROSC patients may have transient ST-elevation on EKG Consider performing serial EKGs. Post-ROSC patients should preferentially be transported to centers capable of managing STEMI, whenever possible

sections.title: Key Documentation
points: ' Immediate post-arrest rhythms, vital signs (pulse, blood pressure, respiratory rate, neurologic status assessment) and oxygen saturation
' Post-ROSC 12-lead EKG

EMR: Scene safety, primary survey, CPR, AED, BVM, bleeding control, oral airway, O2 therapy, epinephrine auto-injector for anaphylaxis, naloxone unit-dose auto-injector
EMT: EMR scope PLUS: NPA, supraglottic airway, CPAP, pulse oximetry, 12-lead ECG acquisition, oral glucose, aspirin, patient own nitroglycerin, albuterol SVN, IM epinephrine, blood glucose monitoring, cervical collar, traction splint
AEMT: EMT scope PLUS: waveform capnography, IO access, IV access initiation, IV/IO fluids, IV medications (dextrose, naloxone, epinephrine, glucagon, analgesics, antiemetics), subcutaneous/intranasal medications, nitroglycerin from stock
Paramedic: AEMT scope PLUS: ET intubation, RSI, cricothyrotomy, needle decompression, manual defibrillation, cardioversion, transcutaneous pacing, 12-lead ECG interpretation, all antidysrhythmics, vasopressors, all medication routes and classes

deterioration_flags: Patient returned to spontaneous circulation following cardiac arrest resuscitation
scope_notes: EMR scope is the minimum national standard — state/MCA may expand
All interventions subject to local medical director credentialing
Pediatric patients: use weight-based dosing and age-appropriate equipment
Contact medical direction when required by local protocol or for interventions outside standard scope
